narrative: Your law school application was denied because our predictive model, which assesses the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated a 72% chance that you would not pass—far above the threshold we use for admissions decisions. This prediction draws from your academic record, test scores, and background details like your middle-class family income and plans to study full-time, without considering gender or race. The main reasons for this outcome center on your class rankings from earlier years, which stood out as the strongest factors pushing the model toward forecasting failure. Specifically, your position in the second decile for Year 3 grades—well below the typical sixth-decile standing of students who passed the bar—had the biggest influence, signaling to the model a notable dip in performance that often correlates with bar struggles. Your Year 1 ranking in the third decile, compared to the average sixth decile among passers, reinforced this concern as the second-most impactful factor, suggesting early academic challenges that carried weight. On a brighter note, your LSAT score of 30.5 pulled in the opposite direction, helping to lower the failure odds somewhat since it's a key strength relative to what the model expects in tougher cases. Your undergraduate GPA of 3.3 actually exceeds the 3.24 average for bar passers, but it wasn't among the top influences here. While your middle income aligns with over a third of successful students and full-time status matches nearly all of them, these didn't outweigh the ranking signals. Compared to admitted applicants, who typically show stronger early rankings closer to the top half of their classes, your profile appeared riskier for bar success. This isn't a judgment on your potential—many overcome similar starts—but the model prioritizes patterns from past students. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost that positive pull, strengthening your later-year grades through targeted prep or additional coursework, and reapplying with updated transcripts that highlight upward trends. You might also explore programs with bar passage support tailored for full-time students from varied backgrounds, turning these insights into your advantage.
